analysis:
- Strong designs (Heuristics 1–6) share the same effective recipe: Pareto prefiltering → cheap singleton/rect surrogate ranking → small adaptive shortlist → stamped lazy-greedy heap that recomputes exact marginal HV only at the heap top → cached HVs → bounded local-swap refinement. This balances quality (exact HV where needed) and runtime (few exact evaluations).
- Variants trade exploration/diversity vs determinism: Heuristic 1’s stochastic sampled-greedy and Heuristic 3’s farthest‑first seeding improve robustness at modest extra cost; others bias more toward singleton rect scores for speed.
- Common efficiency wins: seeding by single-point rect, limiting pool size proportional to k, stamp-based lazy recompute, and caching set HVs avoid repeated heavy HV calls.
- Repeated inefficiencies seen in weaker heuristics and some implementations:
  - O(N^2) Python loops for dominance and pairwise distances (slow in pure Python); avoid or chunk/vectorize.
  - Large dense Monte‑Carlo dominated matrices (M×N) consume memory/time; chunking or bitset representations and reuse are essential.
  - Frequent creation of heavy Python objects as cache keys (frozenset/tuple conversions) and excessive reallocation; mapping sets to compact integer masks or canonical sorted tuples once reduces overhead.
  - Naïve recomputation of hv(S ∪ {p}) from scratch each time; incremental marginal computations or reuse of dominated-sample masks (bitsets) are far cheaper.
  - Inconsistent/unsafe index mappings (searchsorted misuse) and repeated recomputation of trivial quantities (rect_sizes, normalized surrogates).
- Monte‑Carlo fallbacks need adaptive M tied to D and candidate set size; many heuristics use overly large fixed M or inconsistent RNG seeding, harming reproducibility or adding unnecessary cost.
- Local-swap refinement improves HV but is expensive; bounding iterations and prioritizing outsider candidates by surrogate mitigates cost.
experience: Seed a stamped lazy-greedy heap with normalized singleton rect scores, keep a compact shortlist ∝ k, cache HVs using canonical sorted tuples or bitmasks, and recompute exact marginal only for popped items. Reuse Monte‑Carlo dominated masks (bitsets) to compute marginals incrementally; adapt sample size to D and pool size. Limit and prioritize swap trials. Profile hotspots and avoid O(N^2) Python loops—vectorize or chunk.
current_reflection: Seed a stamped lazy-greedy heap with normalized singleton rect scores, keep a compact shortlist ∝ k, cache HVs using canonical sorted tuples or bitmasks, and recompute exact marginal only for popped items. Reuse Monte‑Carlo dominated masks (bitsets) to compute marginals incrementally; adapt sample size to D and pool size. Limit and prioritize swap trials. Profile hotspots and avoid O(N^2) Python loops—vectorize or chunk.
good_reflections: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates and remove points dominated by the reference. Use lazy greedy with a priority queue storing marginal HV contributions and only recompute when a candidate reaches the top. Cache incremental HV or use per‑point contribution routines instead of full recompute. Combine a cheap surrogate (R2) to shortlist candidates, then refine top ones with exact HV/local swaps. Vectorize mask math and minimize Python loops.

Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.

Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.

Always Pareto‑filter first, then shortlist by single‑point rect volumes plus a lightweight diversity pick. Use a stamped lazy greedy heap with exact HV from a fast C library; cache marginal gains keyed by compact bitmasks. Limit Monte‑Carlo to large‑D fallback with small M. Cap swap neighborhood and parallelize exact evaluations. Vectorize dominance checks and avoid hot Python loops or frequent frozenset creation.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: stamped lazy-greedy, Pareto prefilter, rect‑surrogate, shortlist c·k, HV cache (sorted tuple/bitmask), Monte‑Carlo masks, incremental marginals, bounded swaps, vectorization.

- Advice: Map indices to a compact pool, seed a stamped heap with normalized singleton rect scores, recompute exact marginal hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, keep an adaptive shortlist ∝ k, reuse MC bitset samples for marginals and adapt sample size to D/pool size, cap/prioritize swap trials.

- Avoid: O(N^2) Python loops, frequent frozenset/array rebuilds, recomputing all marginals, unbounded swap/search, reinitializing HV objects per call.

- Explanation: Focus exact HV work on promising candidates, amortize stochastic estimates, use cheap compact keys, and vectorize hot paths to maximize HV per runtime.
